- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	# WebIde — Deployment Handoff Document
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-06-06  
4	**Author:** Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6)  
5	**Status:** Plan approved — ready for implementation  
6	**Branch:** develop → main
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 1. What This Document Is
11	
12	A complete handoff specification for taking WebIde from a read-only local .NET app to a production-ready, auto-deploying competitive coding platform on Hetzner. Any engineer picking this up should be able to implement end-to-end from this document alone.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## 2. Current State
17	
18	| Area | Status |
19	|---|---|
20	| ASP.NET MVC web app | ✅ Working (read-only, no auth) |
21	| PostgreSQL + EF Core | ✅ Fully integrated, migrations exist |
22	| Tailwind CSS + Razor views | ✅ Brutalist design system applied |
23	| Code execution | ❌ Data model exists, no implementation |
24	| Authentication | ❌ None |
25	| Docker / docker-compose | ❌ Not created |
26	| CI/CD | ❌ Not created |
27	| Nginx | ❌ Not created |
28	
29	---
30	
31	## 3. Target Architecture
32	
33	```
34	                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
35	                    │             Hetzner VPS                  │
36	                    │         (4 cores / 8 GB RAM)            │
37	                    │                                          │
38	Internet ──HTTPS──▶ │  Nginx :80/:443                         │
39	                    │      │                                   │
40	                    │      ▼                                   │
41	                    │  webide-app :8080  (ASP.NET MVC)         │
42	                    │      │  ▲                                │
43	                    │   Redis│  │SignalR                       │
44	                    │   queue│  │pub/sub                       │
45	                    │      ▼  │                                │
46	                    │  webide-worker (.NET Worker Service)     │
47	                    │      │                                   │
48	                    │      ▼ Docker socket (DooD)              │
49	                    │  sandbox containers (sibling, ephemeral) │
50	                    │      gcc / python / node                 │
51	                    │                                          │
52	                    │  postgres :5432  redis :6379  seq :5341  │
53	                    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
54	```
55	
56	### Why Docker-out-of-Docker (DooD)?
57	The worker mounts `/var/run/docker.sock` from the host so sandbox containers run as siblings (not children) on the host daemon. This avoids the `--privileged` requirement of Docker-in-Docker and is simpler to operate on a single-node deployment. The worker container itself never has user-facing code; the sandbox containers have zero permissions (see §6).
58	
59	---
60	
61	## 4. Resource Allocation
62	
63	| Service | CPU limit | RAM limit | Notes |
64	|---|---|---|---|
65	| nginx | 0.1 | 64 MB | Reverse proxy only |
66	| webide-app | 0.5 | 512 MB | ASP.NET MVC + SignalR |
67	| webide-worker | 0.3 | 256 MB | Manages containers, doesn't run code |
68	| postgres | 0.5 | 1 GB | Primary datastore |
69	| redis | 0.1 | 128 MB | Queue + sessions + SignalR backplane |
70	| seq | 0.3 | 512 MB | Log aggregation UI |
71	| **sandbox slot 1** | **1.0** | **512 MB** | Per-run limit, enforced by Docker |
72	| **sandbox slot 2** | **1.0** | **512 MB** | Max 2 concurrent at once |
73	| OS overhead | 0.2 | 512 MB | — |
74	| **Total** | **4.0** | **~4 GB** | Leaves ~4 GB headroom |
75	
76	**Dynamic slot calculation** (for future server upgrades):
77	```
78	floor((totalRam - reservedRam) / sandboxMemMb)
79	```
80	Controlled by `Worker__MaxConcurrentSandboxes` environment variable. No code change needed to scale up slots on a bigger server.
81	
82	---
83	
84	## 5. End-to-End Submission Flow
85	
86	```
87	1. User → GET /problems/{id}
88	         └─ Monaco editor (CDN), language picker, Submit button
89	
90	2. User clicks Submit
91	         └─ fetch POST /submissions (JSON: problemId, language, sourceCode)
92	         └─ Antiforgery token validated
93	         └─ Submission record created (status = Pending)
94	         └─ SubmissionJob JSON pushed to Redis list "submissions:queue"
95	         └─ Response: { submissionId: 42 }
96	
97	3. Browser opens SignalR connection
98	         └─ hub.invoke("SubscribeToSubmission", 42)
99	         └─ Status chip shows: PENDING (spinner)
100	
101	4. Worker (BLPOP)
102	         └─ Deserializes SubmissionJob
103	         └─ Acquires SemaphoreSlim slot (blocks if both slots busy)
104	         └─ Updates Submission.Status → Running
105	         └─ Publishes Running event → browser shows: RUNNING
106	
107	5. Worker spawns sandbox container
108	         └─ Writes source to /tmp/webide-src/{id}/solution.{ext}
109	         └─ docker run [see §6 for full flags]
110	         └─ CancellationToken fires at TimeLimitMs + 500ms grace
111	
112	6. Container finishes (or is killed)
113	         └─ Worker collects stdout/stderr/exit code/memory
114	         └─ Evaluates against test cases
115	         └─ Writes ExecutionResult + updates Submission.Status
116	         └─ Publishes result to Redis channel "execution:42"
117	
118	7. RedisSubscriptionService (IHostedService in app)
119	         └─ Receives from "execution:*" pattern sub
120	         └─ Pushes to IHubContext<ExecutionHub>.Clients.Group("submission:42")
121	
122	8. Browser receives ExecutionResult via SignalR
123	         └─ Status chip: ACCEPTED / WRONG_ANSWER / TIME_LIMIT / COMPILE_ERROR
124	         └─ Wall time + memory displayed
125	         └─ Test case pass/fail indicators updated
126	```
127	
128	**Exit codes from sandbox:**
129	- `0` = success (check output)
130	- `1` = runtime error
131	- `2` = compile error (set by wrapper script)
132	
133	---
134	
135	## 6. Sandbox Security Model
136	
137	Every sandbox container runs with these exact Docker flags — **non-negotiable**:
138	
139	```bash
140	docker run --rm \
141	  --network none \           # zero network access
142	  --read-only \              # root filesystem immutable
143	  --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m \    # only writable location
144	  --memory 512m \            # hard memory cap
145	  --cpus 1 \                 # 1 CPU core
146	  --ulimit nproc=50 \        # block fork bombs
147	  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
148	  --cap-drop ALL \           # drop all Linux capabilities
149	  -v /tmp/webide-src/{id}:/code:ro \  # source: read-only
150	  webide-sandbox-{lang} /code/solution.{ext} /code/input.txt
151	```
152	
153	**What code CANNOT do:**
154	- Make network requests (outbound or inbound)
155	- Write to filesystem (except `/tmp`)
156	- Fork more than 50 processes
157	- Escalate privileges
158	- Read other submissions' source files
159	- Communicate with any other container
160	
161	**C/C++ compile strategy:** A `compile-and-run.sh` wrapper inside the gcc image compiles to `/tmp/a.out` then executes it. One container, one invocation. Exit 2 = compile failure. The worker maps exit 2 → `SubmissionStatus.CompileError`.
162	
163	---
164	
165	## 7. Files To Create (Complete List)
166	
167	### Phase 0 — Infrastructure (no dependencies, do first)
168	
169	| File | Description |
170	|---|---|
171	| `.env.template` | All env var documentation; actual `.env` lives on server only |
172	| `docker-compose.yml` | 7-service stack with explicit resource limits |
173	| `nginx/nginx.conf` | Reverse proxy + WebSocket upgrade for `/hubs/` path |
174	| `Dockerfile.app` | Multi-stage: `sdk:10.0` build → `aspnet:10.0` runtime |
175	| `Dockerfile.worker` | Same pattern, targets WebIde.Worker |
176	| `sandbox/gcc.Dockerfile` | `alpine:3.20` + gcc/g++ + entrypoint wrapper |
177	| `sandbox/python.Dockerfile` | `python:3.12-alpine` |
178	| `sandbox/node.Dockerfile` | `node:22-alpine` |
179	| `sandbox/compile-and-run.sh` | gcc compile+run script; exit 2 on compile error |
180	
181	### Phase 1 — Authentication
182	
183	| File | Change |
184	|---|---|
185	| `WebIde.Model/User.cs` | Add `string? GitHubId`, `string? AvatarUrl` |
186	| `WebIde.DAL/Migrations/` | New migration: `AddGitHubFieldsToUser` |
187	| `WebIde.Frontend/WebIde.Frontend.csproj` | Add 9 NuGet packages (see §9) |
188	| `WebIde.Frontend/Program.cs` | Full rewrite: Redis, Session, GitHub OAuth, SignalR, Health, Serilog |
189	| `WebIde.Frontend/Repositories/UserRepository.cs` | Add `GetByGitHubIdAsync`, `UpsertGitHubUserAsync` |
190	| `WebIde.Frontend/Controllers/AuthController.cs` | NEW: `/auth/github/login` + `/auth/logout` |
191	| `WebIde.Frontend/Views/Shared/_Layout.cshtml` | Replace hardcoded avatar with login/logout conditional |
192	| `WebIde.Frontend/Views/Shared/_Sidebar.cshtml` | Replace `ROOT_USER` with `User.Identity.Name` |
193	
194	### Phase 2 — Worker Service
195	
196	| File | Description |
197	|---|---|
198	| `WebIde.Worker/WebIde.Worker.csproj` | Worker SDK; refs Model + DAL; Docker.DotNet, Redis, Serilog |
199	| `WebIde.Worker/Program.cs` | `IDbContextFactory`, `DockerClient` (unix socket), service registrations |
200	| `WebIde.Worker/Models/SubmissionJob.cs` | Queue message contract (record) |
201	| `WebIde.Worker/Models/SandboxResult.cs` | Execution output model |
202	| `WebIde.Worker/Models/SubmissionResultEvent.cs` | Redis pub/sub payload |
203	| `WebIde.Worker/Models/WorkerOptions.cs` | Typed config from `Worker:*` env vars |
204	| `WebIde.Worker/Services/SandboxOrchestrator.cs` | `SemaphoreSlim` + Docker.DotNet lifecycle |
205	| `WebIde.Worker/Services/SubmissionEvaluator.cs` | Output normalization + test case comparison |
206	| `WebIde.Worker/Workers/SubmissionWorker.cs` | `BackgroundService`: BLPOP → execute → evaluate → persist → publish |
207	| `WebIde.slnx` | Add Worker project reference |
208	
209	### Phase 3 — Submission Endpoint + SignalR + Editor
210	
211	| File | Change |
212	|---|---|
213	| `WebIde.Frontend/Hubs/ExecutionHub.cs` | NEW: `[Authorize]`, `SubscribeToSubmission(int id)` |
214	| `WebIde.Frontend/Services/RedisSubscriptionService.cs` | NEW: `IHostedService`, Redis → SignalR bridge |
215	| `WebIde.Frontend/Controllers/SubmissionController.cs` | Add `POST [Authorize]` endpoint |
216	| `WebIde.Frontend/Views/Problem/Details.cshtml` | Monaco editor + submit form + SignalR JS |
217	
218	### Phase 5 — CI/CD
219	
220	| File | Description |
221	|---|---|
222	| `.github/workflows/ci.yml` | Build + test gate (runs on PR to main) |
223	| `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` | Build images → push ghcr.io → SSH deploy → blue-green |
224	
225	---
226	
227	## 8. GitHub Actions Deployment Pipeline
228	
229	### CI Gate (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)
230	Triggers: push to `develop`, PR to `main`
231	
232	```
233	checkout → setup-dotnet 10.x → dotnet restore → dotnet build → dotnet test
234	```
235	
236	Branch protection rule on `main`: this workflow must pass before merge.
237	
238	### Deploy Pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`)
239	Triggers: push to `main`
240	
241	```
242	1. Login to ghcr.io (GITHUB_TOKEN)
243	2. Build + push ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/app:sha-{SHA} + :latest
244	3. Build + push ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/worker:sha-{SHA} + :latest
245	4. SSH into Hetzner (appleboy/ssh-action):
246	   a. cd /opt/webide
247	   b. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose pull webide-app webide-worker
248	   c. Run migrations:
249	      docker run --rm --env-file .env --network webide_webide-net \
250	        ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/app:sha-{SHA} \
251	        dotnet ef database update --no-build
252	   d. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose up -d webide-app webide-worker
253	   e. Poll GET http://localhost/health  (30 attempts × 2s = 60s timeout)
254	   f. ✅ Pass: docker image prune -f
255	   g. ❌ Fail: IMAGE_TAG=latest docker compose up -d (rollback)
256	```
257	
258	**Required GitHub Secrets:**
259	| Secret | Value |
260	|---|---|
261	| `HETZNER_HOST` | Server IP address |
262	| `HETZNER_USER` | SSH username (e.g. `deploy`) |
263	| `HETZNER_SSH_KEY` | Private SSH key (RSA or Ed25519) |
264	
265	**Blue-green mechanism:** `IMAGE_TAG` env var controls which image Docker Compose uses. The old container keeps serving until the health check confirms the new container is healthy. On failure, `IMAGE_TAG=latest` restores the last known-good image (`:latest` is only promoted after a successful deploy).
266	
267	**Important:** The migration `docker run` must use `--network webide_webide-net` so it can reach the `postgres` service by hostname.
268	
269	---
270	
271	## 9. NuGet Packages to Add
272	
273	### `WebIde.Frontend.csproj`
274	```xml
275	<PackageReference Include="AspNet.Security.OAuth.GitHub" Version="9.*" />
276	<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Cookies" Version="10.*" />
277	<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.SignalR" Version="10.*" />
278	<PackageReference Include="StackExchange.Redis" Version="2.*" />
279	<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.StackExchangeRedis" Version="9.*" />
280	<PackageReference Include="Serilog.AspNetCore" Version="9.*" />
281	<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Seq" Version="8.*" />
282	<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Console" Version="6.*" />
283	<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.NpgSql" Version="9.*" />
284	<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.Redis" Version="9.*" />
285	```
286	
287	### `WebIde.Worker.csproj`
288	```xml
289	<PackageReference Include="Docker.DotNet" Version="3.*" />
290	<PackageReference Include="StackExchange.Redis" Version="2.*" />
291	<PackageReference Include="Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL" Version="9.*" />
292	<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design" Version="9.*" PrivateAssets="all" />
293	<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Extensions.Hosting" Version="9.*" />
294	<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Seq" Version="8.*" />
295	<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Console" Version="6.*" />
296	```
297	
298	---
299	
300	## 10. Key Implementation Notes
301	
302	### `Program.cs` middleware order (critical)
303	```csharp
304	app.UseAuthentication();   // must be before Authorization
305	app.UseAuthorization();
306	app.UseSession();
307	app.MapHub<ExecutionHub>("/hubs/execution");
308	app.MapHealthChecks("/health");
309	app.MapControllerRoute(...);
310	```
311	
312	### `IDbContextFactory` in Worker (critical)
313	The `SubmissionWorker` is a **singleton** (hosted service). `WebIdeDbContext` is **scoped**. Injecting it directly causes a DI lifetime exception at startup. Use `IDbContextFactory<WebIdeDbContext>` and call `factory.CreateDbContext()` per job.
314	
315	### Memory stats collection order (critical)
316	Set `AutoRemove = false` when creating the sandbox container. Collect memory via `InspectContainerAsync` **before** calling `RemoveContainerAsync`. After removal, stats are gone.
317	
318	### Redis SignalR backplane (important)
319	Register with `.AddSignalR().AddStackExchangeRedis(connectionString)`. Even on a single instance this is free and makes horizontal scaling possible later without a code change.
320	
321	### Monaco Editor (CDN, no npm)
322	The project has no JS build pipeline. Use AMD loader from CDN:
323	```html
324	<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/monaco-editor@0.47.0/min/vs/loader.js"></script>
325	```
326	Sync Monaco value to a hidden `<textarea name="sourceCode">` before form submission.
327	
328	### nginx WebSocket timeout
329	```nginx
330	location /hubs/ {
331	    proxy_pass http://webide-app:8080;
332	    proxy_http_version 1.1;
333	    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
334	    proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
335	    proxy_read_timeout 86400;  # critical — prevents SignalR long-poll drops
336	}
337	```
338	
339	---
340	
341	## 11. Server Setup (One-Time, Manual)
342	
343	Run on the Hetzner server before first deploy:
344	
345	```bash
346	# Install Docker
347	curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
348	
349	# Create deploy user
350	useradd -m -s /bin/bash deploy
351	usermod -aG docker deploy
352	
353	# Create app directory
354	mkdir -p /opt/webide
355	chown deploy:deploy /opt/webide
356	
357	# Create source temp dir (worker bind mount)
358	mkdir -p /tmp/webide-src
359	chmod 777 /tmp/webide-src
360	
361	# Copy .env from template (fill in secrets)
362	cp .env.template /opt/webide/.env
363	chmod 600 /opt/webide/.env
364	
365	# Pull sandbox images (one-time)
366	docker pull webide-sandbox-gcc     # or build locally and push to ghcr.io
367	docker pull webide-sandbox-python
368	docker pull webide-sandbox-javascript
369	
370	# Add SSH public key for deploy user (GitHub Actions will use this)
371	mkdir -p /home/deploy/.ssh
372	echo "YOUR_PUBLIC_KEY" >> /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
373	chmod 700 /home/deploy/.ssh
374	chmod 600 /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
375	```
376	
377	**Note:** Sandbox images (`webide-sandbox-*`) should also be built and pushed to ghcr.io as part of a one-time setup step — they don't change with app deploys, only when language versions need updating.
378	
379	---
380	
381	## 12. GitHub OAuth App Setup
382	
383	1. Go to GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → OAuth Apps → New OAuth App
384	2. **Application name:** WebIde
385	3. **Homepage URL:** `https://YOUR_DOMAIN`
386	4. **Authorization callback URL:** `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/auth/github/callback`
387	5. Copy Client ID and Client Secret → add to `/opt/webide/.env`
388	
389	---
390	
391	## 13. Verification Checklist
392	
393	### Infrastructure smoke test
394	- [ ] `docker compose up postgres redis seq -d` starts cleanly
395	- [ ] `dotnet run` (WebIde.Frontend) → `/health` returns `{"status":"Healthy"}`
396	- [ ] Seq UI accessible at `http://localhost:5341`
397	
398	### Sandbox isolation test
399	```bash
400	# Create test source
401	mkdir -p /tmp/test-src
402	echo 'import os; os.system("ls /")' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
403	
404	docker run --rm --network none --read-only \
405	  --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m --memory 512m --cpus 1 \
406	  --ulimit nproc=50 --security-opt no-new-privileges --cap-drop ALL \
407	  -v /tmp/test-src:/code:ro \
408	  webide-sandbox-python python /code/solution.py
409	# Should produce empty output or error — NOT a directory listing
410	```
411	
412	### Worker integration test
413	```bash
414	redis-cli RPUSH submissions:queue \
415	  '{"SubmissionId":1,"ProblemId":1,"Language":"python","SourceCode":"print(input())","TimeLimitMs":2000,"MemoryLimitKb":524288}'
416	# Check Seq for worker logs
417	# Check DB: SELECT status, wall_time_ms FROM submissions WHERE id = 1;
418	```
419	
420	### Full flow test
421	- [ ] Login via GitHub OAuth → user record created in DB
422	- [ ] Submit correct Python solution → ACCEPTED, wall time displayed
423	- [ ] Submit infinite loop → TIME_LIMIT_EXCEEDED after ~2s
424	- [ ] Submit wrong output → WRONG_ANSWER
425	- [ ] Submit C++ with syntax error → COMPILE_ERROR with stderr shown
426	- [ ] Submit while 2 jobs are running → third queues, executes when slot frees
427	- [ ] `/health` with postgres stopped → returns 503
428	- [ ] `/health` with redis stopped → returns 503
429	
430	### CI/CD test
431	- [ ] Open PR to main with broken build → CI fails, merge blocked
432	- [ ] Push to main → images built, pushed to ghcr.io, deployed to Hetzner
433	- [ ] Simulate unhealthy app → rollback to `:latest` triggers
434	
435	---
436	
437	## 14. Risks & Mitigations
438	
439	| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
440	|---|---|---|
441	| Sandbox container escape via Docker socket | Medium | Worker uses named images only; sandbox containers have `--network none` + `--cap-drop ALL` — cannot reach the socket |
442	| `/tmp/webide-src` stale dirs on crash | Medium | `finally` block in orchestrator always deletes. Add startup cleanup of dirs older than 5 min |
443	| Memory stats unavailable after removal | Medium | `AutoRemove = false`; inspect before `RemoveContainerAsync` |
444	| EF migration fails mid-deploy | Low | Migration runs before `compose up`; non-zero exit halts deploy, old containers keep serving |
445	| SignalR falls back to long-poll | Low | `proxy_read_timeout 86400` on nginx; long-poll still works, just slower |
446	| Both sandbox slots occupied indefinitely | By design | Redis queue serializes work; jobs wait until a slot frees |
447	| DooD requires `/var/run/docker.sock` present | Low | Document in server setup; standard on any Docker host |
448	
449	---
450	
451	## 15. Future Work (Post-POC)
452	
453	| Feature | Notes |
454	|---|---|
455	| HTTPS / Let's Encrypt | Certbot sidecar + nginx SSL config once domain confirmed |
456	| Per-test-case execution | Run sandbox once per test case for granular TLE/MLE per case |
457	| Redis Streams queue | Upgrade from BLPOP list for consumer groups + replay |
458	| Horizontal worker scaling | Remove in-process semaphore, use Redis for distributed slot tracking |
459	| Admin UI | Problem + test case creation (currently no write UI beyond submissions) |
460	| Rate limiting | Max N submissions per user per time window |
461	| `WebIde.Tests` project | Currently no test project; CI `dotnet test` passes vacuously |
462	| Sandbox image CI | Build sandbox images in a separate workflow triggered by `sandbox/**` changes |
463	| Seq access hardening | Put Seq behind nginx basic auth or VPN; don't expose on public port |
464

file.content:
# WebIde — Deployment Handoff Document

**Date:** 2026-06-06  
**Author:** Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6)  
**Status:** Plan approved — ready for implementation  
**Branch:** develop → main

---

## 1. What This Document Is

A complete handoff specification for taking WebIde from a read-only local .NET app to a production-ready, auto-deploying competitive coding platform on Hetzner. Any engineer picking this up should be able to implement end-to-end from this document alone.

---

## 2. Current State

| Area | Status |
|---|---|
| ASP.NET MVC web app | ✅ Working (read-only, no auth) |
| PostgreSQL + EF Core | ✅ Fully integrated, migrations exist |
| Tailwind CSS + Razor views | ✅ Brutalist design system applied |
| Code execution | ❌ Data model exists, no implementation |
| Authentication | ❌ None |
| Docker / docker-compose | ❌ Not created |
| CI/CD | ❌ Not created |
| Nginx | ❌ Not created |

---

## 3. Target Architecture

```
                    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │             Hetzner VPS                  │
                    │         (4 cores / 8 GB RAM)            │
                    │                                          │
Internet ──HTTPS──▶ │  Nginx :80/:443                         │
                    │      │                                   │
                    │      ▼                                   │
                    │  webide-app :8080  (ASP.NET MVC)         │
                    │      │  ▲                                │
                    │   Redis│  │SignalR                       │
                    │   queue│  │pub/sub                       │
                    │      ▼  │                                │
                    │  webide-worker (.NET Worker Service)     │
                    │      │                                   │
                    │      ▼ Docker socket (DooD)              │
                    │  sandbox containers (sibling, ephemeral) │
                    │      gcc / python / node                 │
                    │                                          │
                    │  postgres :5432  redis :6379  seq :5341  │
                    └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Why Docker-out-of-Docker (DooD)?
The worker mounts `/var/run/docker.sock` from the host so sandbox containers run as siblings (not children) on the host daemon. This avoids the `--privileged` requirement of Docker-in-Docker and is simpler to operate on a single-node deployment. The worker container itself never has user-facing code; the sandbox containers have zero permissions (see §6).

---

## 4. Resource Allocation

| Service | CPU limit | RAM limit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| nginx | 0.1 | 64 MB | Reverse proxy only |
| webide-app | 0.5 | 512 MB | ASP.NET MVC + SignalR |
| webide-worker | 0.3 | 256 MB | Manages containers, doesn't run code |
| postgres | 0.5 | 1 GB | Primary datastore |
| redis | 0.1 | 128 MB | Queue + sessions + SignalR backplane |
| seq | 0.3 | 512 MB | Log aggregation UI |
| **sandbox slot 1** | **1.0** | **512 MB** | Per-run limit, enforced by Docker |
| **sandbox slot 2** | **1.0** | **512 MB** | Max 2 concurrent at once |
| OS overhead | 0.2 | 512 MB | — |
| **Total** | **4.0** | **~4 GB** | Leaves ~4 GB headroom |

**Dynamic slot calculation** (for future server upgrades):
```
floor((totalRam - reservedRam) / sandboxMemMb)
```
Controlled by `Worker__MaxConcurrentSandboxes` environment variable. No code change needed to scale up slots on a bigger server.

---

## 5. End-to-End Submission Flow

```
1. User → GET /problems/{id}
         └─ Monaco editor (CDN), language picker, Submit button

2. User clicks Submit
         └─ fetch POST /submissions (JSON: problemId, language, sourceCode)
         └─ Antiforgery token validated
         └─ Submission record created (status = Pending)
         └─ SubmissionJob JSON pushed to Redis list "submissions:queue"
         └─ Response: { submissionId: 42 }

3. Browser opens SignalR connection
         └─ hub.invoke("SubscribeToSubmission", 42)
         └─ Status chip shows: PENDING (spinner)

4. Worker (BLPOP)
         └─ Deserializes SubmissionJob
         └─ Acquires SemaphoreSlim slot (blocks if both slots busy)
         └─ Updates Submission.Status → Running
         └─ Publishes Running event → browser shows: RUNNING

5. Worker spawns sandbox container
         └─ Writes source to /tmp/webide-src/{id}/solution.{ext}
         └─ docker run [see §6 for full flags]
         └─ CancellationToken fires at TimeLimitMs + 500ms grace

6. Container finishes (or is killed)
         └─ Worker collects stdout/stderr/exit code/memory
         └─ Evaluates against test cases
         └─ Writes ExecutionResult + updates Submission.Status
         └─ Publishes result to Redis channel "execution:42"

7. RedisSubscriptionService (IHostedService in app)
         └─ Receives from "execution:*" pattern sub
         └─ Pushes to IHubContext<ExecutionHub>.Clients.Group("submission:42")

8. Browser receives ExecutionResult via SignalR
         └─ Status chip: ACCEPTED / WRONG_ANSWER / TIME_LIMIT / COMPILE_ERROR
         └─ Wall time + memory displayed
         └─ Test case pass/fail indicators updated
```

**Exit codes from sandbox:**
- `0` = success (check output)
- `1` = runtime error
- `2` = compile error (set by wrapper script)

---

## 6. Sandbox Security Model

Every sandbox container runs with these exact Docker flags — **non-negotiable**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  --network none \           # zero network access
  --read-only \              # root filesystem immutable
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m \    # only writable location
  --memory 512m \            # hard memory cap
  --cpus 1 \                 # 1 CPU core
  --ulimit nproc=50 \        # block fork bombs
  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  --cap-drop ALL \           # drop all Linux capabilities
  -v /tmp/webide-src/{id}:/code:ro \  # source: read-only
  webide-sandbox-{lang} /code/solution.{ext} /code/input.txt
```

**What code CANNOT do:**
- Make network requests (outbound or inbound)
- Write to filesystem (except `/tmp`)
- Fork more than 50 processes
- Escalate privileges
- Read other submissions' source files
- Communicate with any other container

**C/C++ compile strategy:** A `compile-and-run.sh` wrapper inside the gcc image compiles to `/tmp/a.out` then executes it. One container, one invocation. Exit 2 = compile failure. The worker maps exit 2 → `SubmissionStatus.CompileError`.

---

## 7. Files To Create (Complete List)

### Phase 0 — Infrastructure (no dependencies, do first)

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `.env.template` | All env var documentation; actual `.env` lives on server only |
| `docker-compose.yml` | 7-service stack with explicit resource limits |
| `nginx/nginx.conf` | Reverse proxy + WebSocket upgrade for `/hubs/` path |
| `Dockerfile.app` | Multi-stage: `sdk:10.0` build → `aspnet:10.0` runtime |
| `Dockerfile.worker` | Same pattern, targets WebIde.Worker |
| `sandbox/gcc.Dockerfile` | `alpine:3.20` + gcc/g++ + entrypoint wrapper |
| `sandbox/python.Dockerfile` | `python:3.12-alpine` |
| `sandbox/node.Dockerfile` | `node:22-alpine` |
| `sandbox/compile-and-run.sh` | gcc compile+run script; exit 2 on compile error |

### Phase 1 — Authentication

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `WebIde.Model/User.cs` | Add `string? GitHubId`, `string? AvatarUrl` |
| `WebIde.DAL/Migrations/` | New migration: `AddGitHubFieldsToUser` |
| `WebIde.Frontend/WebIde.Frontend.csproj` | Add 9 NuGet packages (see §9) |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Program.cs` | Full rewrite: Redis, Session, GitHub OAuth, SignalR, Health, Serilog |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Repositories/UserRepository.cs` | Add `GetByGitHubIdAsync`, `UpsertGitHubUserAsync` |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Controllers/AuthController.cs` | NEW: `/auth/github/login` + `/auth/logout` |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Views/Shared/_Layout.cshtml` | Replace hardcoded avatar with login/logout conditional |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Views/Shared/_Sidebar.cshtml` | Replace `ROOT_USER` with `User.Identity.Name` |

### Phase 2 — Worker Service

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `WebIde.Worker/WebIde.Worker.csproj` | Worker SDK; refs Model + DAL; Docker.DotNet, Redis, Serilog |
| `WebIde.Worker/Program.cs` | `IDbContextFactory`, `DockerClient` (unix socket), service registrations |
| `WebIde.Worker/Models/SubmissionJob.cs` | Queue message contract (record) |
| `WebIde.Worker/Models/SandboxResult.cs` | Execution output model |
| `WebIde.Worker/Models/SubmissionResultEvent.cs` | Redis pub/sub payload |
| `WebIde.Worker/Models/WorkerOptions.cs` | Typed config from `Worker:*` env vars |
| `WebIde.Worker/Services/SandboxOrchestrator.cs` | `SemaphoreSlim` + Docker.DotNet lifecycle |
| `WebIde.Worker/Services/SubmissionEvaluator.cs` | Output normalization + test case comparison |
| `WebIde.Worker/Workers/SubmissionWorker.cs` | `BackgroundService`: BLPOP → execute → evaluate → persist → publish |
| `WebIde.slnx` | Add Worker project reference |

### Phase 3 — Submission Endpoint + SignalR + Editor

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `WebIde.Frontend/Hubs/ExecutionHub.cs` | NEW: `[Authorize]`, `SubscribeToSubmission(int id)` |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Services/RedisSubscriptionService.cs` | NEW: `IHostedService`, Redis → SignalR bridge |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Controllers/SubmissionController.cs` | Add `POST [Authorize]` endpoint |
| `WebIde.Frontend/Views/Problem/Details.cshtml` | Monaco editor + submit form + SignalR JS |

### Phase 5 — CI/CD

| File | Description |
|---|---|
| `.github/workflows/ci.yml` | Build + test gate (runs on PR to main) |
| `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` | Build images → push ghcr.io → SSH deploy → blue-green |

---

## 8. GitHub Actions Deployment Pipeline

### CI Gate (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)
Triggers: push to `develop`, PR to `main`

```
checkout → setup-dotnet 10.x → dotnet restore → dotnet build → dotnet test
```

Branch protection rule on `main`: this workflow must pass before merge.

### Deploy Pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`)
Triggers: push to `main`

```
1. Login to ghcr.io (GITHUB_TOKEN)
2. Build + push ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/app:sha-{SHA} + :latest
3. Build + push ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/worker:sha-{SHA} + :latest
4. SSH into Hetzner (appleboy/ssh-action):
   a. cd /opt/webide
   b. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose pull webide-app webide-worker
   c. Run migrations:
      docker run --rm --env-file .env --network webide_webide-net \
        ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/app:sha-{SHA} \
        dotnet ef database update --no-build
   d. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose up -d webide-app webide-worker
   e. Poll GET http://localhost/health  (30 attempts × 2s = 60s timeout)
   f. ✅ Pass: docker image prune -f
   g. ❌ Fail: IMAGE_TAG=latest docker compose up -d (rollback)
```

**Required GitHub Secrets:**
| Secret | Value |
|---|---|
| `HETZNER_HOST` | Server IP address |
| `HETZNER_USER` | SSH username (e.g. `deploy`) |
| `HETZNER_SSH_KEY` | Private SSH key (RSA or Ed25519) |

**Blue-green mechanism:** `IMAGE_TAG` env var controls which image Docker Compose uses. The old container keeps serving until the health check confirms the new container is healthy. On failure, `IMAGE_TAG=latest` restores the last known-good image (`:latest` is only promoted after a successful deploy).

**Important:** The migration `docker run` must use `--network webide_webide-net` so it can reach the `postgres` service by hostname.

---

## 9. NuGet Packages to Add

### `WebIde.Frontend.csproj`
```xml
<PackageReference Include="AspNet.Security.OAuth.GitHub" Version="9.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.Authentication.Cookies" Version="10.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.SignalR" Version="10.*" />
<PackageReference Include="StackExchange.Redis" Version="2.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Caching.StackExchangeRedis" Version="9.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.AspNetCore" Version="9.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Seq" Version="8.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Console" Version="6.*" />
<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.NpgSql" Version="9.*" />
<PackageReference Include="AspNetCore.HealthChecks.Redis" Version="9.*" />
```

### `WebIde.Worker.csproj`
```xml
<PackageReference Include="Docker.DotNet" Version="3.*" />
<PackageReference Include="StackExchange.Redis" Version="2.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL" Version="9.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design" Version="9.*" PrivateAssets="all" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Extensions.Hosting" Version="9.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Seq" Version="8.*" />
<PackageReference Include="Serilog.Sinks.Console" Version="6.*" />
```

---

## 10. Key Implementation Notes

### `Program.cs` middleware order (critical)
```csharp
app.UseAuthentication();   // must be before Authorization
app.UseAuthorization();
app.UseSession();
app.MapHub<ExecutionHub>("/hubs/execution");
app.MapHealthChecks("/health");
app.MapControllerRoute(...);
```

### `IDbContextFactory` in Worker (critical)
The `SubmissionWorker` is a **singleton** (hosted service). `WebIdeDbContext` is **scoped**. Injecting it directly causes a DI lifetime exception at startup. Use `IDbContextFactory<WebIdeDbContext>` and call `factory.CreateDbContext()` per job.

### Memory stats collection order (critical)
Set `AutoRemove = false` when creating the sandbox container. Collect memory via `InspectContainerAsync` **before** calling `RemoveContainerAsync`. After removal, stats are gone.

### Redis SignalR backplane (important)
Register with `.AddSignalR().AddStackExchangeRedis(connectionString)`. Even on a single instance this is free and makes horizontal scaling possible later without a code change.

### Monaco Editor (CDN, no npm)
The project has no JS build pipeline. Use AMD loader from CDN:
```html
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/monaco-editor@0.47.0/min/vs/loader.js"></script>
```
Sync Monaco value to a hidden `<textarea name="sourceCode">` before form submission.

### nginx WebSocket timeout
```nginx
location /hubs/ {
    proxy_pass http://webide-app:8080;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
    proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
    proxy_read_timeout 86400;  # critical — prevents SignalR long-poll drops
}
```

---

## 11. Server Setup (One-Time, Manual)

Run on the Hetzner server before first deploy:

```bash
# Install Docker
curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh

# Create deploy user
useradd -m -s /bin/bash deploy
usermod -aG docker deploy

# Create app directory
mkdir -p /opt/webide
chown deploy:deploy /opt/webide

# Create source temp dir (worker bind mount)
mkdir -p /tmp/webide-src
chmod 777 /tmp/webide-src

# Copy .env from template (fill in secrets)
cp .env.template /opt/webide/.env
chmod 600 /opt/webide/.env

# Pull sandbox images (one-time)
docker pull webide-sandbox-gcc     # or build locally and push to ghcr.io
docker pull webide-sandbox-python
docker pull webide-sandbox-javascript

# Add SSH public key for deploy user (GitHub Actions will use this)
mkdir -p /home/deploy/.ssh
echo "YOUR_PUBLIC_KEY" >> /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 700 /home/deploy/.ssh
chmod 600 /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
```

**Note:** Sandbox images (`webide-sandbox-*`) should also be built and pushed to ghcr.io as part of a one-time setup step — they don't change with app deploys, only when language versions need updating.

---

## 12. GitHub OAuth App Setup

1. Go to GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → OAuth Apps → New OAuth App
2. **Application name:** WebIde
3. **Homepage URL:** `https://YOUR_DOMAIN`
4. **Authorization callback URL:** `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/auth/github/callback`
5. Copy Client ID and Client Secret → add to `/opt/webide/.env`

---

## 13. Verification Checklist

### Infrastructure smoke test
- [ ] `docker compose up postgres redis seq -d` starts cleanly
- [ ] `dotnet run` (WebIde.Frontend) → `/health` returns `{"status":"Healthy"}`
- [ ] Seq UI accessible at `http://localhost:5341`

### Sandbox isolation test
```bash
# Create test source
mkdir -p /tmp/test-src
echo 'import os; os.system("ls /")' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py

docker run --rm --network none --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m --memory 512m --cpus 1 \
  --ulimit nproc=50 --security-opt no-new-privileges --cap-drop ALL \
  -v /tmp/test-src:/code:ro \
  webide-sandbox-python python /code/solution.py
# Should produce empty output or error — NOT a directory listing
```

### Worker integration test
```bash
redis-cli RPUSH submissions:queue \
  '{"SubmissionId":1,"ProblemId":1,"Language":"python","SourceCode":"print(input())","TimeLimitMs":2000,"MemoryLimitKb":524288}'
# Check Seq for worker logs
# Check DB: SELECT status, wall_time_ms FROM submissions WHERE id = 1;
```

### Full flow test
- [ ] Login via GitHub OAuth → user record created in DB
- [ ] Submit correct Python solution → ACCEPTED, wall time displayed
- [ ] Submit infinite loop → TIME_LIMIT_EXCEEDED after ~2s
- [ ] Submit wrong output → WRONG_ANSWER
- [ ] Submit C++ with syntax error → COMPILE_ERROR with stderr shown
- [ ] Submit while 2 jobs are running → third queues, executes when slot frees
- [ ] `/health` with postgres stopped → returns 503
- [ ] `/health` with redis stopped → returns 503

### CI/CD test
- [ ] Open PR to main with broken build → CI fails, merge blocked
- [ ] Push to main → images built, pushed to ghcr.io, deployed to Hetzner
- [ ] Simulate unhealthy app → rollback to `:latest` triggers

---

## 14. Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Sandbox container escape via Docker socket | Medium | Worker uses named images only; sandbox containers have `--network none` + `--cap-drop ALL` — cannot reach the socket |
| `/tmp/webide-src` stale dirs on crash | Medium | `finally` block in orchestrator always deletes. Add startup cleanup of dirs older than 5 min |
| Memory stats unavailable after removal | Medium | `AutoRemove = false`; inspect before `RemoveContainerAsync` |
| EF migration fails mid-deploy | Low | Migration runs before `compose up`; non-zero exit halts deploy, old containers keep serving |
| SignalR falls back to long-poll | Low | `proxy_read_timeout 86400` on nginx; long-poll still works, just slower |
| Both sandbox slots occupied indefinitely | By design | Redis queue serializes work; jobs wait until a slot frees |
| DooD requires `/var/run/docker.sock` present | Low | Document in server setup; standard on any Docker host |

---

## 15. Future Work (Post-POC)

| Feature | Notes |
|---|---|
| HTTPS / Let's Encrypt | Certbot sidecar + nginx SSL config once domain confirmed |
| Per-test-case execution | Run sandbox once per test case for granular TLE/MLE per case |
| Redis Streams queue | Upgrade from BLPOP list for consumer groups + replay |
| Horizontal worker scaling | Remove in-process semaphore, use Redis for distributed slot tracking |
| Admin UI | Problem + test case creation (currently no write UI beyond submissions) |
| Rate limiting | Max N submissions per user per time window |
| `WebIde.Tests` project | Currently no test project; CI `dotnet test` passes vacuously |
| Sandbox image CI | Build sandbox images in a separate workflow triggered by `sandbox/**` changes |
| Seq access hardening | Put Seq behind nginx basic auth or VPN; don't expose on public port |

Specifically, I need to know:

1. **Project structure**: List the .NET projects (csproj files), solution file (.slnx), and how they reference each other. Look for WebIde.Frontend, WebIde.Model, WebIde.DAL, WebIde.Worker.

PROJECT STRUCTURE - Solution & Project References **Solution file:** - `/Users/fbeleta/Documents/Web-ide/Web_ide/WebIde.slnx` **Projects:** - `WebIde.Console` — Console app for data population and LINQ demonstrations (not a package service) - `WebIde.Model` — Data model entities (no .DAL suffix in folder name) - `WebIde.DAL` — Entity Framework Core context and migrations - `WebIde.Frontend` — ASP.NET MVC web layer (this IS the main app, not called WebIde.Web but uses Web namespace) - `WebIde.Api` — Directory exists but is **empty** (only bin/obj folders, no .csproj) **Project references (from csproj files):** - `WebIde.Frontend` references: `WebIde.Model`, `WebIde.DAL` - `WebIde.DAL` references: `WebIde.Model` - `WebIde.Console` references: `WebIde.Model` (not listed, but imports it) ### 2. CURRENT Program.cs / Startup (WebIde.Frontend) **File:** `/Users/fbeleta/Documents/Web-ide/Web_ide/WebIde.Frontend/Program.cs` **Services registered:** ```csharp builder.Services.AddControllersWithViews(); builder.Services.AddDbContext(options => options.UseNpgsql( builder.Configuration.GetConnectionString("WebIdeDb"), o => o.MigrationsAssembly("WebIde.DAL"))); builder.Services.AddScoped(); builder.Services.AddScoped(); builder.Services.AddScoped(); builder.Services.AddScoped(); builder.Services.AddScoped(); builder.Services.AddScoped(); ``` **Middleware order:** 1. Default route pattern: `{controller=Home}/{action=Index}/{id?}` **Auth setup:** ❌ **NONE** — No authentication/authorization services configured. `UseAuthorization()` is called but no corresponding authentication middleware or services. ### 3. lastPrompt: i want you to please review the plan in /docs/deployment-handoff.md and review if there are any holes in the plan or if its too shallow. ask as many questions as needed

aiTitle: Review deployment handoff plan for completeness

content.text:
Excellent — I have a complete picture of the current state. The plan's claims about what exists vs. doesn't are all accurate. Now let me think through holes and gaps before asking the most important questions.

I've identified ~30+ gaps. Before I write up the full review, I need to understand your priorities so I focus on what matters.

questions.question: What's the actual target audience and stakes for this deployment?

options.label: POC / demo only
options.description: Throwaway demo. Few users (you, reviewers, maybe a class). Data loss tolerable. Plan's current depth is roughly right.

options.label: Real users, low traffic
options.description: Students or a small competitive group actually use it. Data must persist. Downtime annoying but tolerable. Needs HTTPS, backups, rate limiting.

options.label: Production-grade
options.description: Treat as if it'll handle hostile users and untrusted code in the open. Full security posture, monitoring, incident response.

questions.question: Is HTTPS truly 'future work' or required from day 1?

options.label: Required day 1 (Recommended)
options.description: GitHub OAuth callback in §12 is already https://YOUR_DOMAIN/... — cookies need Secure flag, OAuth state cookie won't work over HTTP. Plan §15 says 'future' which contradicts §12.

options.label: Plain HTTP first, HTTPS later
options.description: Accept that OAuth won't actually work until HTTPS is added. Plan is consistent if §12 changes to http://.

options.label: No public domain yet
options.description: Deploying behind IP/tailscale/VPN, no public hostname. Different security profile entirely.

questions.question: How should I deliver the review?

options.label: Annotated gap list (Recommended)
options.description: I produce a critique document: list each hole, severity, recommended fix. You decide which to fold into the handoff doc.

options.label: Edit the handoff doc directly
options.description: I patch the deployment-handoff.md itself with all the missing sections and corrections. Higher friction to review.

options.label: Both — list first, then I edit
options.description: I write the gap list now, you approve, then I edit the handoff doc with the agreed fixes.

questions.question: Anything you already know is shallow or missing that I should be sure to cover?

options.label: Sandbox security depth
options.description: Worried the §6 sandbox model isn't tight enough (pids-limit, non-root user, etc.).

options.label: Output / test-case judging logic
options.description: Whitespace, floating point, multi-test-case execution model is vague.

options.label: Rollback / migration safety
options.description: Worried the GH Actions blue-green + EF migration flow has holes if something fails mid-deploy.

options.label: Operational / day-2 (backups, logs, monitoring)
options.description: No backup, log rotation, disk capacity, or alerting story.

options.label: I want a fresh perspective
options.description: Don't bias me — flag what you find.

aiTitle: Review deployment handoff plan for completeness

content.text: Got it. Production-grade, HTTPS day 1, prioritize sandbox/judging/rollback. Let me write the gap analysis to the plan file now.

input.content:
# Review of `docs/deployment-handoff.md`

## Context

The user has a deployment handoff document at `docs/deployment-handoff.md` written by a prior Claude session, describing how to take WebIde from a read-only local .NET app to a production-ready deployment on a Hetzner VPS. The user has asked for a gap analysis: are there any holes, is any section too shallow, and what would a production-grade implementer trip over?

The user clarified:
- **Stakes:** Production-grade. Real hostile users may submit untrusted code.
- **HTTPS:** Required from day 1 (the current doc punts it to "Future Work" but §12 already lists `https://` callback URLs — internally inconsistent).
- **Priority gap areas:** Sandbox security depth, judging / test-case logic, rollback / migration safety.
- **Deliverable:** Gap list first (this document), then I edit `docs/deployment-handoff.md` with the agreed fixes.

I verified the plan's "current state" claims against the actual codebase — all twelve claims (no Docker, no auth, no Worker project, no tests, `ROOT_USER` hardcoded in `_Sidebar.cshtml`, etc.) match reality. So the gaps below are about depth and missing topics, not factual errors in the inventory.

---

## Overall Verdict

The plan is **structurally sound but operationally shallow for a production-grade deployment**. It is good as an architecture sketch and as a phase-by-phase file inventory; it would let an experienced implementer get the happy path standing up. But it leaves several load-bearing topics — sandbox PID limits, output normalization, blue-green rollback correctness, DataProtection key persistence, Redis auth, secrets management, log/backup retention — either unspecified, hand-waved, or quietly punted to "Future Work" despite being prerequisites for the stated goals.

The most dangerous category is **silent inconsistencies between sections**: §12 assumes HTTPS while §15 punts HTTPS to future work; §8 step 2 pushes `:latest` *before* the health check, but step g claims rollback uses `:latest`; §6 uses `--ulimit nproc` (which is per-UID, not per-container) where `--pids-limit` is what's actually wanted. An implementer following the plan literally will ship broken behavior in all three cases.

---

## Critical Gaps (block production-grade, fix before any deployment)

### C1. HTTPS contradicts itself across the doc
- §12 instructs the implementer to register the GitHub OAuth app with `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/auth/github/callback`.
- §15 lists "HTTPS / Let's Encrypt" under **Future Work**.
- ASP.NET Core OAuth state cookie defaults to `SameSite=None` + `Secure=true` after the redirect — over plain HTTP, the callback will silently drop the cookie and OAuth will fail with a state validation error. Cookie auth (`.AddCookie`) similarly needs `Secure=Always` in production. Without HTTPS day 1, **auth does not work at all**.
- **Fix:** Move HTTPS to Phase 0. Add a section spelling out: certbot strategy (sidecar container vs. host install), nginx SSL config (cert path, HSTS header, redirect 80→443), cookie policy (`Secure`, `SameSite=Lax`, `HttpOnly`), `app.UseHsts()` + `app.UseHttpsRedirection()` in `Program.cs`, and `ForwardedHeaders` middleware so the app sees `X-Forwarded-Proto=https` from nginx (otherwise `Request.Scheme` is `http` and auth redirects break).

### C2. DataProtection keys are not persisted
- ASP.NET Core uses DataProtection keys for cookie auth, antiforgery tokens, and OAuth state. By default they live in the container's filesystem under a generated path.
- On container restart (every deploy), keys are regenerated → every user is logged out, every active antiforgery token becomes invalid, every in-flight OAuth callback fails.
- With Redis already in the stack, this is a 3-line fix and the plan omits it entirely.
- **Fix:** Add to `Program.cs` section: `services.AddDataProtection().PersistKeysToStackExchangeRedis(redis, "DataProtection-Keys").SetApplicationName("WebIde")`. List the Redis key under expected state in §10.

### C3. Redis has no authentication
- §4 lists Redis with no `requirepass`. §11 server setup doesn't set one. Diagram in §3 shows `redis:6379` as if it's a service like postgres — which it is, but it's also (a) the SignalR backplane, (b) the submission queue, and (c) the session store. Anyone with shell on the box (or a misconfigured Docker network) can read sessions, inject submissions, and impersonate the backplane.
- **Fix:** Add `REDIS_PASSWORD` to `.env.template`, set `requirepass` in the redis service command, append `,password={REDIS_PASSWORD}` to all client connection strings (Frontend, Worker). Bind redis to `127.0.0.1:6379` on the host or, better, do not publish the port at all and only let it be reachable on the internal `webide-net` Docker network.

### C4. Database port and Seq UI exposed by default
- The diagram in §3 shows `postgres :5432` and `seq :5341` as if they're addressable. §16 future-work concedes "Seq access hardening" but doesn't change the default. Production needs these locked down day 1.
- **Fix:** In `docker-compose.yml` spec, explicitly say `ports:` is omitted for postgres/redis (internal-only via Docker network); Seq either gated behind nginx basic auth + HTTPS or only reachable via SSH tunnel. Add a note: "If you need to inspect Postgres, use `docker compose exec postgres psql` — do not publish 5432."

### C5. Sandbox: `--ulimit nproc=50` is the wrong tool
- `nproc` is a per-UID rlimit applied at the host level. Inside a container that shares a UID with the host (e.g., everything inside runs as root → host UID 0), the limit aggregates across containers and the host. With `--cap-drop ALL` you still get fork bombs up to nproc, and worse, you can exhaust the *host's* nproc budget for that UID.
- The correct tool is `--pids-limit 64` (cgroup pids controller, per-container).
- The plan also doesn't run sandbox processes as a non-root in-container user. `--user nobody:nogroup` is defense in depth that costs nothing.
- **Fix:** Replace `--ulimit nproc=50` with `--pids-limit 64`. Add `--user nobody:nogroup` to the docker run flags. Bake a `nobody` user into each sandbox Dockerfile. Add a verification test in §13 that runs `:(){ :|:& };:` (bash fork bomb, or python equivalent) and confirms the container dies without disturbing the host.

### C6. Blue-green rollback is broken as written
- §8 step 2 pushes both `:sha-{SHA}` AND `:latest`.
- §8 step g says rollback uses `IMAGE_TAG=latest`.
- But `:latest` was just overwritten with the failing image in step 2, *before* the health check in step e. So the rollback restores the broken image.
- **Fix:** Gate the `:latest` tag push behind a successful health check. Use a two-step push: build+push `:sha-{SHA}`, deploy, verify health, then promote `:latest` only on success. On failure, `:latest` still points to the previous good build. Alternatively, capture the previous `:latest` digest at the start of the deploy and pin to that digest on rollback.

### C7. EF migration failure mid-deploy has no recovery path
- §8 step c runs `dotnet ef database update` *before* `compose up` (step d). Risk table §14 says "non-zero exit halts deploy, old containers keep serving."
- But what about the inverse: migration **succeeds**, then health check fails. The old image doesn't know about the new schema. Rollback (per C6, even when fixed) brings back code that may now crash at startup or corrupt data because columns are NOT NULL or constraints differ.
- This is the classic expand-contract problem. The plan acknowledges nothing about it.
- **Fix:** Add a "Migration Discipline" section: all migrations must be additive and backwards-compatible with the previous deployed app version (add nullable columns, never rename/drop in a single deploy; use a two-deploy pattern for destructive changes). Document that the implementer is responsible for verifying this. Alternative for a POC scope: accept that rollback after a migration is manual (restore DB backup → redeploy old image), and write that out explicitly.

### C8. Postgres has no backup story
- "Production-grade" + single-VPS + ~1 GB Postgres budget + zero backup configuration = guaranteed data loss event.
- **Fix:** Add `restic` or `pg_dump` to a cron container (e.g., `prodrigestivill/postgres-backup-local` is one-line in compose). State retention policy. Specify where backups go — local disk is not a backup; need an offsite target (Hetzner Storage Box, S3-compatible, or B2). Without offsite, a host failure is total loss.

### C9. Worker has no liveness / graceful shutdown story
- Worker is a `BackgroundService` doing `BLPOP` in a loop. Plan doesn't specify:
  - **SIGTERM behavior:** On `docker compose up -d webide-worker` (redeploy), the old container gets a SIGTERM. If a sandbox is mid-execution, the worker container is killed at the 10s default grace period. The submission row is left as `Running` forever and the Docker container it spawned may be orphaned (it's a sibling, not a child — DooD consequence).
  - **Worker health:** No health endpoint, no liveness probe. If the worker crashes or BLPOP hangs, the queue silently grows and submissions accumulate as `Pending` with no alarm.
  - **Stuck submissions reaper:** Nothing to transition `Running` rows older than `TimeLimitMs + grace` to `InternalError`.
- **Fix:** Define `IHostApplicationLifetime` handling — on stopping, stop accepting new BLPOP and drain in-flight job (or mark it `InternalError` after a deadline). Add a heartbeat key in Redis written by the worker each loop (`worker:heartbeat = now`); add a health check on the Frontend that fails if heartbeat older than 30s. Define a startup-time reaper that scans for `Running` submissions older than max-time-limit + N seconds and marks them failed.

### C10. Judging logic is dangerously vague
- §6 says "compile-and-run.sh wrapper" and §10/§7 says "SubmissionEvaluator: output normalization + test case comparison." That is the entirety of the judging spec.
- Open questions with material UX impact:
  - **Trailing whitespace / newline:** strip or preserve? Most online judges trim trailing whitespace per line and trailing blank lines but preserve internal whitespace. Plan doesn't say.
  - **Line endings:** CRLF (Windows submissions) vs LF — normalize? Sandbox is Alpine Linux but source may be authored on any OS.
  - **Floating point tolerance:** Problems with float answers (per the `DifficultyLevel` and `Problem` model there's no `OutputType` field) — exact match or epsilon? If exact, 99% of float problems are unjudgeable.
  - **Multi-test-case execution model:** Plan implies "Worker spawns sandbox container" (single, per submission) and "evaluates against test cases" (plural). Does the wrapper iterate test cases inside one container? Spawn one container per test case? `TestCase.InputArgs` is named singular — is it stdin, argv, or a file? Plan §6's `docker run` shows `/code/input.txt` as a positional arg, which doesn't match either argv or stdin conventions cleanly.
  - **Partial credit / per-case status:** `Submission.Score` exists. Is it the sum of per-test points (`TestCase.Points`)? All-or-nothing? Plan doesn't say.
  - **Output truncation:** A solution that writes 10 GB to stdout — capped where? Worker memory? Container `--tmpfs` is 64 MB, stdout is streamed not buffered. Need a stdout size cap with `head -c` or equivalent.
- **Fix:** Add §6a "Judging contract" with explicit rules for whitespace, line endings, float tolerance, multi-test-case model (recommended: one container per submission; wrapper reads test cases from `/code/cases.json` and emits a results JSON; worker parses it), partial credit formula, stdout/stderr size caps (4 MB each, truncate-with-marker), and the exit-code-to-status mapping table.

---

## Important Gaps (should fix; production-grade but not deploy-blockers)

### I1. CSRF / antiforgery for the submission endpoint
- §5 says "Antiforgery token validated" for the JSON POST. But fetch POST with `Content-Type: application/json` bypasses default antiforgery (which expects form data). The plan needs to specify either: (a) send token in `RequestVerificationToken` header and use `IAntiforgery.ValidateRequestAsync`, or (b) rely on SameSite cookies + CORS as the CSRF defense and document that as the chosen model.
- **Fix:** Add to §10: exact antiforgery wiring for JSON POST + Razor token emission pattern.

### I2. Cookie policy not specified
- No mention of `Secure`, `SameSite`, `HttpOnly`, expiration, sliding vs absolute. With GitHub OAuth, the external cookie needs `SameSite=Lax` minimum or the callback drops the correlation cookie. Plan needs an explicit cookie policy block.
- **Fix:** Add a `services.ConfigureApplicationCookie` snippet in §10 with explicit settings.

### I3. nginx config under-specified for SignalR + OAuth
- §10 nginx block only covers `/hubs/`. Missing:
  - `proxy_set_header Host $host;`
  - `proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;`
  - `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;`
  - `proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;` ← required for ASP.NET Core to see https
  - Client max body size for source code uploads (default 1 MB — fine here, but state it)
  - Gzip / brotli for static assets
  - Rate limiting zones for `/auth/`, `/submissions`
- **Fix:** Expand the nginx config block with all of the above.

### I4. Secrets inventory is implicit
- Plan mentions `.env.template` but never lists what's *in* it. Implementer has to reverse-engineer from §10/§8/§12. Missing for sure: `REDIS_PASSWORD`, `POSTGRES_PASSWORD`, `POSTGRES_USER`, `POSTGRES_DB`, `GITHUB_CLIENT_ID`, `GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET`, `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT`, `SEQ_API_KEY` (if Seq auth is enabled), `DATAPROTECTION_KEY_RING_REDIS_KEY`, `WORKER__MAXCONCURRENTSANDBOXES`, `WORKER__SANDBOXMEMMB`, `PUBLIC_HOSTNAME` (for OAuth redirects), `LETSENCRYPT_EMAIL`.
- **Fix:** Add a §9a "Secret inventory" subsection that enumerates every env var with: name, owner (app/worker/postgres/redis), example, security class (secret vs config).

### I5. Rate limiting day 1, not "future work"
- A production-grade competitive coding platform with public GitHub OAuth is an obvious target for abusive submission spam (compute exhaustion), enumeration, and OAuth callback flooding.
- Plan says "Rate limiting" is future work. ASP.NET Core 7+ has `AddRateLimiter` built in — minutes to add.
- **Fix:** Add to §10: rate limiter config — fixed window on `/submissions` (e.g., 5/min per user), token bucket on `/auth/*` (10/min per IP). Move from §15 to Phase 1.

### I6. Source-dir handling: race conditions and information leak
- §11 sets `chmod 777 /tmp/webide-src` on the host. Any local user can read every submission's source. On a Hetzner VPS with only a `deploy` user, this is low risk, but it's a code smell for a production-grade plan.
- §14 mentions "stale dirs on crash" with cleanup older than 5 min. But the 5-min cleanup can race the worker — submission with timeout 4m59s, cleanup walks the dir at 5m, races a still-running sandbox.
- **Fix:** Create `/tmp/webide-src` mode 700, owned by the deploy user (which is the same user the worker container runs as). Have the orchestrator track active submission IDs in-memory and skip them in the cleanup pass. Document the cleanup as "older than max-time-limit + 1 minute" not a fixed 5 min.

### I7. Memory and wall-time measurement specifics
- §10 says "Collect memory via `InspectContainerAsync` before `RemoveContainerAsync`" — but `InspectContainerAsync` returns `ContainerInspectResponse` which doesn't include memory stats. Memory stats come from `GetContainerStatsAsync` (a streaming endpoint). And on cgroupv2 (Docker 20.10+ default), `memory.peak` is the file to read; older cgroupv1 used `memory.max_usage_in_bytes`. The Docker.DotNet wrapper varies in what it exposes.
- Wall time: measured where? Including container startup? On a cold sandbox image, the first pull/create adds 100-500 ms. If included, fast solutions look slow.
- **Fix:** Specify: wall time = (container exit timestamp − container start timestamp) measured from Docker daemon, **not** wall-clock around `docker run`. Peak memory = stream stats until exit and take max(`memory.peak`/`memory.max_usage_in_bytes`). Add a unit/integration test that submits a fixed-cost program and asserts measurement is within bounds.

### I8. Worker DI lifetime note is correct but partial
- §10 mentions `IDbContextFactory` (good). Doesn't mention that `DockerClient` should be a singleton (it is in the recommended Docker.DotNet pattern) and that `IConnectionMultiplexer` (StackExchange.Redis) must be a singleton (well-known footgun if registered scoped).
- **Fix:** Expand §10 with a "DI lifetime cheat sheet" listing `IConnectionMultiplexer` singleton, `DockerClient` singleton, `WebIdeDbContext` scoped via factory, `SandboxOrchestrator` singleton, `SubmissionEvaluator` scoped.

### I9. Phase numbering is broken
- §7 has Phase 0, 1, 2, 3, then jumps to Phase 5. No Phase 4. Reader will assume something is missing.
- **Fix:** Renumber, or call Phase 4 "Wiring & smoke test" if there's intended local-dev work between feature complete and CI/CD.

### I10. No acceptance criteria per phase
- "Phase 1 — Authentication" has a file list, no "this phase is done when…" criteria. Implementer has no contract for handoff between phases.
- **Fix:** Add a one-line acceptance criterion per phase (e.g., Phase 1: "User can log in via GitHub OAuth, sees their handle in the sidebar, and a row exists in `users` with their `GitHubId`").

### I11. Sandbox images: who builds, where stored, when updated
- §11 says "Pull sandbox images (one-time)" with `docker pull webide-sandbox-*`, but there's no registry path. §15 future-work says "Build sandbox images in a separate workflow." So how do they get there for day 1?
- **Fix:** Either (a) build them as part of `deploy.yml` if `sandbox/**` changed, push to ghcr.io with the same `:sha-{SHA}` tagging; or (b) document a one-time manual `docker build && docker push` to ghcr.io with explicit image names. The deploy pipeline must pin a digest, not `:latest`, otherwise sandbox behavior can change silently between deploys.

### I12. Verification §13 lacks adversarial cases
- Checklist is happy-path. Missing for production-grade:
  - Fork bomb resistance test
  - Memory bomb test (allocate 2 GB → MLE)
  - Filesystem write outside `/tmp` → permission denied
  - Outbound network request → no route to host
  - `/proc/self/status` inspection → still root inside? (after fix C5, should be `nobody`)
  - Long stdout (write 100 MB) → truncated, not OOM on host
  - Submitting while worker is being redeployed → submission queues, not lost
  - GitHub OAuth: invalid state cookie → user-friendly error, not 500
- **Fix:** Add an "Adversarial verification" subsection with these tests as numbered steps.

---

## Minor Gaps / Nits

### N1. Resource table sums to exactly 4.0 CPU, no headroom
- Both sandbox slots at 1.0 CPU + everything else exactly = 4.0. Under burst load expect CFS throttling on the app/worker. Either reduce sandbox to 0.9 CPU, or be explicit that throttling is acceptable.

### N2. Sandbox memory cap vs `Problem.MemoryLimitKb`
- Problem has a per-problem memory limit (Kb). Sandbox is hardcoded 512 MB. Which wins? Probably `min(Problem.MemoryLimitKb, sandboxCap)`. Plan doesn't say.

### N3. Compile-error mapping for Python/Node
- §5 says "exit 2 = compile error (set by wrapper script)." Python has no compile step; a `SyntaxError` exits with code 1 and a stderr trace. Plan should clarify that for Python/Node, a runtime parse error → `SubmissionStatus.RuntimeError`, not `CompileError`, and that's intentional. Or have the wrapper detect Python syntax errors specifically and re-exit as 2.

### N4. Tailwind via CDN in production
- Current `_Layout.cshtml` uses Tailwind Play CDN (`cdn.tailwindcss.com`). This is documented by Tailwind as **not for production**. It adds a 300+ KB JS dependency on every page load and a SPOF. Plan doesn't address.
- **Fix:** Add to Phase 0 or Phase 3: switch to a pre-built CSS file. Either run Tailwind CLI as part of the app Dockerfile build stage (multi-stage with `node:22-alpine` builder layer), or generate `site.tailwind.css` locally and commit it. Same problem applies to Monaco loader (CDN is fine for monaco — it's designed for it; Tailwind Play is not).

### N5. Health check definition
- `/health` is referenced everywhere but never specified. Production-grade needs split liveness/readiness, and need to choose what's a hard fail (DB down → 503) vs soft (Seq down → 200 with degraded). Currently §13 says `/health` returns 503 when postgres or redis is down — fine, but unspecified.

### N6. Log retention / rotation
- Seq + Docker `json-file` driver default = uncapped. On an 8 GB box this fills the disk in weeks under load.
- **Fix:** Set `logging.driver: json-file` with `max-size: 10m` and `max-file: 3` in every compose service. Set Seq retention to e.g., 14 days.

### N7. ProblemSet / Organization / Tag in scope?
- The data model has Organizations, ProblemSets, Tags. None are mentioned in any phase. Are they admin-managed via raw SQL for v1? Out of scope for deploy? Plan should say "v1 ships with seeded data and no admin UI; ProblemSet/Org/Tag are read-only until Phase 6."

### N8. Local dev workflow undocumented
- New dev clones the repo — what's the first-run command? Plan §13 lists `docker compose up postgres redis seq -d` then `dotnet run`. State this as the canonical dev loop. Where do they get a `.env` for local? (Hint: `.env.template` with local-safe defaults.)

### N9. Monaco language sync
- §10 says Monaco loader from CDN. Doesn't mention the language picker → Monaco model language mapping (`cpp`, `python`, `javascript`), or that Submit must sync the editor content to the hidden textarea **and** include the language. Minor but trips up implementers.

### N10. Branch protection mentioned but not configured
- §8 says "Branch protection rule on `main`: this workflow must pass before merge" — but configuring branch protection on GitHub is a manual step that's easy to forget. Add to a §11a "GitHub repo setup" checklist along with the OAuth app.

### N11. `WORKER__MAXCONCURRENTSANDBOXES` slot math
- §4 has the floor() formula but doesn't acknowledge sandbox CPU. If you have 16 GB / 512 MB = 32 slots by RAM, but only 4 cores, you'd thrash. Slot calc should be `min(floor(ram/sandboxRam), floor((cpu - reservedCpu) / sandboxCpu))`.

### N12. SignalR backplane is load-bearing, not optional
- §10 says "Even on a single instance this is free." Misleading — the worker is a *separate process*, so the bridge from Redis pub/sub to SignalR groups is the only way the browser hears about the result. It's not "in case you scale later," it's the architecture.

---

## What's Genuinely Good in the Plan (don't lose in the edit)

- Clear DooD vs DinD reasoning with a non-evasive justification.
- Explicit `docker run` flag list in §6 with explanations — this is exactly the level of detail security-sensitive code needs.
- Phase 0 / infrastructure-first ordering is correct (often plans start with code).
- Resource allocation table — even if numbers are tight, the discipline of accounting for every container is good.
- Risks & Mitigations table — the *format* is right; just shallow in content.
- File-by-file inventory in §7 is the most useful part of the document.

---

## Proposed Edit List for `docs/deployment-handoff.md`

Mapping each gap above to a concrete change in the handoff doc. Severity: **C** = critical (deploy-blocker), **I** = important, **N** = nit.

| # | Sev | Section to edit | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | C | §15 → Phase 0 | Add new §11b "HTTPS / TLS" — certbot strategy, nginx SSL block, HSTS, ForwardedHeaders middleware, cookie Secure. Remove HTTPS from Future Work. |
| C2 | C | §10 | Add "DataProtection persistence" block with Redis snippet. |
| C3 | C | §4, §10, §11 | Add `REDIS_PASSWORD`, set `requirepass`, update connection strings. Remove redis port publish. |
| C4 | C | §3 diagram, compose spec in §7 | Note postgres/redis internal-only; Seq behind nginx basic auth. |
| C5 | C | §6 | Replace `--ulimit nproc=50` with `--pids-limit 64`. Add `--user nobody:nogroup` + Dockerfile change in §7. |
| C6 | C | §8 | Restructure pipeline: don't push `:latest` until after health check passes; capture previous `:latest` digest for rollback. |
| C7 | C | New §10a | "Migration discipline": additive only, two-deploy pattern for destructive changes, manual rollback procedure with backup restore. |
